sourceMovie.title: Ray
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Ray (2004) stuck because it commits to its drama premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Taylor Hackford's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The drama and music blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: A Star Is Born
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ray clicked for you, A Star Is Born (2018) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and music, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ray clicked for you, Rocketman (2019) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and music, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Ray?
faq.answer: Try A Star Is Born, Rocketman, The Legend of 1900—each shares DNA with Ray while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Ray?
faq.answer: Ray (2004) is best described as Drama, Music. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Ray worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
